sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.content:
Ultraviolet radiation exposure: Strong (approximately 90% attributable fraction in white individuals). Detail: UV-R exposure is believed to be related to approximately 90% of melanomas in white individuals [MS-6]. Includes both natural sunlight and artificial tanning bed exposure. Intermittent intense sun exposure and sunburn history are associated with superficial spreading and nodular melanoma subtypes; chronic sun exposure is associated with lentigo maligna and desmoplastic subtypes [MS-2,7]. Tanning bed use has significantly increased melanoma risk worldwide, with approximately 2.85-fold higher risk in users, showing a dose-response relationship [4,19,20,70].

Indoor tanning bed use: Strong (dose-response relationship established). Detail: Population-based studies show approximately 2.85-fold higher risk of melanoma in tanning bed users [70]. Risk increases with number of exposures and years of use [19,20]. Earlier age at first use is associated with earlier onset of disease [20]. Molecular analyses show nearly 2-fold increased mutation burden of melanocytes in tanning bed users compared to non-users [70].

Multiple blistering sunburns: Moderate to Strong. Detail: ≥5 blistering sunburns between age 15 and 20 years is associated with increased risk of superficial spreading and nodular melanoma subtypes [7,23]. Sunburn history plays an established role as a melanoma risk factor [6,23].

Phenotypic predisposition: Strong. Detail: Risk factors include atypical moles/dysplastic nevi (serving as risk markers for melanoma developing elsewhere), increased mole count (particularly large nevi), difficulty tanning, light skin tone/Fitzpatrick skin type I, red hair, blue eyes, high tendency to develop freckles, and pheomelanin-predominant phenotype [3,6,52,53, MS-3]. Atypical/dysplastic nevi are larger, with more color variation than typical nevi, and share some clinical features with melanoma [3].

Male sex: Moderate. Detail: Male sex is a recognized risk factor for melanoma development [1, MS-3]. Males also have higher mortality from melanoma.

Older age: Moderate. Detail: Age greater than 50 years is associated with increased melanoma risk [1, MS-3]. Older age is associated with higher melanoma-specific mortality despite lower rates of sentinel lymph node positivity [239].

Personal history of melanoma: Strong. Detail: Previous cutaneous melanoma places an individual at high risk for developing additional primary melanomas. Approximately 5% of patients without a family history develop subsequent melanomas, compared to approximately 10% of those with a family history [62-64, MS-5].

Personal history of non-melanoma skin cancers: Strong. Detail: Patients with basal cell carcinoma or squamous cell carcinoma have approximately 17-fold increased relative risk for developing melanoma compared to control populations [55].

Personal history of childhood cancer: Moderate. Detail: Childhood cancer survivors have a 2.5-fold increased risk of developing melanoma, with a median latency of 21 years from initial diagnosis [10].

Immunosuppression: Strong. Detail: Solid organ transplant recipients have significantly increased melanoma risk, with higher melanoma-specific mortality compared to non-recipients [11,58]. Hematopoietic cell transplant recipients have increased melanoma risk [12]. HIV/AIDS is associated with significantly increased melanoma risk [13]. Hematologic malignancies (chronic lymphocytic leukemia/small lymphocytic lymphoma, non-Hodgkin lymphoma) also increase risk [8,9].

Family history of melanoma: Moderate to Strong. Detail: Family history of melanoma increases melanoma risk. Family or personal history of ≥2 invasive cutaneous melanomas warrants genetic counseling consideration. Approximately 10% of melanomas can be linked to hereditary factors including germline pathogenic variants [61, MS-5].

Germline pathogenic variants (CDKN2A, CDK4, BAP1, others): Moderate to Strong (implied). Detail: Germline pathogenic variants predisposing to familial melanoma include CDKN2A, CDK4, MC1R, BAP1 (especially for uveal melanoma), TERT, POT1, and MITF. Other cancer predisposition genes with increased melanoma risk include TP53, PTEN, CHEK2, BRCA1/2, BLM, and ATM [6,15-17,66,67, ME-A1]. Patients with known high-risk PV in the family (especially CDKN2A, CD
